coredata.dc:title: Solid-state photoluminescent silicone-carbon dots/dendrimer composites for highly efficient luminescent solar concentrators
coredata.dc:creator: Wu, Jun
Xin, Wei
Wu, Yuhan
Zhan, Yuan
Li, Jinhua
Wang, Jianying
Huang, Shiqiang
Wang, Xianbao
coredata.dc:description: Luminescent solar concentrators (LSCs) with high performances depend on not only the luminescent materials, but their dispersed polymer matrixes. However, carbon dots (CDs)-based LSCs suffer from aggregation-caused fluorescence quenching with the increased concentration of CDs in polymer matrix. To solve this issue, a novel solid-state photoluminescent (PL) silicone-carbon dots (Si-CDs) composites was synthesized by one-step solvothermal treatment of citric acid, dicyandiamide and 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTES). The solid-state PL Si-CDs showed yellow-green emission at 525 nm under excitation of UV light. For better dispersion of Si-CDs, two types of silicone dendrimers (denoted as D1 and D2) were designed and synthesized via a transesterification and hydrosilylation reaction method. The structure differences between D1 and D2 are the contents of phenyl and methoxy groups. Remarkably, silicone dendrimers with high loading of Si-CDs (up to 35 wt%) could be obtained owing to their good compatibility. Single-layered LSCs could be prepared easily by directly casting the above mixtures on glass substrates followed by room temperature curing. The external optical efficiency (η opt) of 7.58%, 5.38% had been achieved from D1 and D2 based LSCs (25 × 25 × 1 mm), respectively. This work provides a convenient method for preparing highly efficient LSCs, posing great potential in various specific applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Silicone-carbon dots
Solid-state photoluminescence
Silicone dendrimer
Luminescent solar concentrators
External optical efficiency

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast footnotes grantnumber grantsponsor highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The illustration for the composition and architecture of LSCs based on silicone-carbon dots/dendrimers composites.
Schematic illustration for the synthesis of Si-CDs by one-step solvothermal approach.

(a, b) FESEM and TEM images of Si-CDs. (c) PL decay spectra of Si-CDs excited at 450 nm. (d) The UV–vis absorption and PL spectra (excitation and emission) the of Si-CDs, and the photographs for the Si-CDs powder under natural light (left) and 365 nm UV light (right) in the inset.
Schematic illustration for the synthesis of silicone dendrimers (D1 and D2) by transesterification and hydrosilylation reaction.
Schematic illustration for the preparation process of LSCs by casting mixture of Si-CDs and silicone dendrimers on glass substrate following by room temperature curing.
(a, b) Photographs of D1 and D2 based LSCs with various Si-CDs content from 0 to 35 wt% under natural light and 365 nm of UV light, respectively.

(a, b) Transmittance spectra of D1 and D2 based LSCs with various Si-CDs content from 0 to 35 wt%. (c, d) Photoluminescence spectra of D1 and D2-based LSCs with various Si-CDs content from 0 to 35 wt%.

(a, b) Relationship of the photocurrent density and photovoltage (J–V) of the D1 and D2 based LSCs (25 × 25 × 1 mm), respectively. (c, d) The corresponding external optical efficiencies histogram of D1 and D2 based LSCs (25 × 25 × 1 mm), respectively. The error bar is obtained from the highest and lowest values based on three measurements for each sample.

head.ce:title: Solid-state photoluminescent silicone-carbon dots/dendrimer composites for highly efficient luminescent solar concentrators

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory for the Green Preparation and Application of Functional Materials, Ministry of Education, Hubei Key Laboratory of Polymer Materials, School of Materials Science and Engineering, Hubei University, Wuhan 430062, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory for the Green Preparation and Application of Functional Materials
Hubei Key Laboratory of Polymer Materials
School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory for the Green Preparation and Application of Functional Materials, Ministry of Education, Hubei Key Laboratory of Polymer Materials, School of Materials Science and Engineering, Hubei University, Wuhan 430062, PR China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at. Hubei University, Wuhan, China.

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed this work equally.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Luminescent solar concentrators (LSCs) with high performances depend on not only the luminescent materials, but their dispersed polymer matrixes. However, carbon dots (CDs)-based LSCs suffer from aggregation-caused fluorescence quenching with the increased concentration of CDs in polymer matrix. To solve this issue, a novel solid-state photoluminescent (PL) silicone-carbon dots (Si-CDs) composites was synthesized by one-step solvothermal treatment of citric acid, dicyandiamide and 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTES). The solid-state PL Si-CDs showed yellow-green emission at 525 nm under excitation of UV light. For better dispersion of Si-CDs, two types of silicone dendrimers (denoted as D1 and D2) were designed and synthesized via a transesterification and hydrosilylation reaction method. The structure differences between D1 and D2 are the contents of phenyl and methoxy groups. Remarkably, silicone dendrimers with high loading of Si-CDs (up to 35 wt%) could be obtained owing to their good compatibility. Single-layered LSCs could be prepared easily by directly casting the above mixtures on glass substrates followed by room temperature curing. The external optical efficiency (η opt) of 7.58%, 5.38% had been achieved from D1 and D2 based LSCs (25 × 25 × 1 mm), respectively. This work provides a convenient method for preparing highly efficient LSCs, posing great potential in various specific applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Luminescent solar concentrator (LSC) is a new type of light concentrating device, which can offer significant potential for solar energy capture in the urban environment. Its superiority of light weight, easy tunability in color and processability, ability to work under diffuse light has received considerable attentions [1–2]. The basic architecture of a LSC consists of a transparent waveguide substrate and luminescent species. The luminescent species can be directly dispersed in the waveguide substrate or coated on the surface of the waveguide substrate. Thanks to this architecture, sunlight can be absorbed by luminescent species, which in turn re-emits light at longer wavelengths in a waveguide substrate. Due to the complete internal reflection, the emitted photons are trapped in the waveguide and transmitted to the edge of the waveguide substrate. The light collected by large-area devices can be redirected and harvested onto small-area photovoltaic cells [3–6]. The optical efficiency of LSC depends not only on the photophysical properties of the emitting materials, but also on the properties of the waveguide materials [7]. Development of high efficiency emitters and their matched matrixes is highly desired.

To date, several luminescent materials such as organic dyes [8–9], metal nanoclusters [10], quantum dots [11], aggregation induced emission molecules [12–13], perovskite nanocrystals [14], etc., have been used as luminescent species. Among these, carbon dots (CDs) have the specific characteristics, including easy synthesis, low cost, green and non-toxic, high light stability and high photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) [13,15–18]. Owing to these features, CDs-LSCs had been developed rapidly in the past five years [13,18–21]. Gong et al., first designed N-doped carbon-dots (N-CDs) with high PLQY, and further fabricated N-CDs/PMMA thin film LSCs, the external optical efficiency (η opt) can reach 4.75% [19]. Recently, Zhao et al., reported highly efficient large-area LSCs (100–225 cm2) based on CDs of a high PLQYs of 65%, and an external optical efficiency of 2.2% had been achieved [22]. However, there still exists the aggregation-caused fluorescence quenching of CDs in the liquid and solid-state applications [23–26]. In general, the CDs concentration in LSCs is limited to less than 10 wt% owing to the aggregation-caused fluorescence quenching effect. To solve that, formation of solid-state PL CDs, e.g., anchoring CDs in solid or crystal, is an effective method to conquer the aggregation-caused fluorescence quenching of CDs [27–28]. In addition, the poor dispersion of CDs in waveguide substrate is another key problem, limiting the development of high performance LSCs. To date, conventional polymers, such as PMMA or PVP, etc., had been used as waveguide substrates of LSCs. To further solve the compatibility of polymer matrixes and luminescent species, several new waveguide materials such as unsaturated polyester [29], polylactic acid [30], sol–gel glasses including silica-metallic oxide [31], and organic–inorganic hybrids including silicone materials [32–34] and di-urethane-silica [1,35] have been proposed as potential alternatives to PMMA and PVP. Specially, silicon-containing materials are chosen as waveguide materials because of their unique properties [36], such as flexibility, chemical resistance, and thermal stability [37]. For instance, Griffini et al., first synthesized Lumogen Red (LR305)-silica core–shell nanoparticles as new nanostructured luminophore by the sol–gel process. To better dispersing LR305-silica core–shell nanoparticles, a two-component silicone resin namely Dow Silgard 184 was used as waveguide materials in the LSC devices, thus the maximum external photon efficiency as high as 2.12% was achieved [34]. Besides, Kaniyoor et al., also used Lumogen Red (LR305) as the luminophore and di-ureasil organic–inorganic hybrid material as the waveguide materials for LSCs [1,38–39]. The di-ureasil waveguide materials offered several advantages than organic waveguide materials, especially extension of the absorption window through energy transfer. Although great progress in luminescent species and waveguide substrates of LSCs had been achieved, the matching issue between the emitters and the waveguide materials is critical to further improve the performance of the LSCs.

In this work, to solve the above issues including the aggregation-caused fluorescence quenching in the current CDs-based LSCs and compatibility between luminescent species and waveguide substrates, a novel kind of solid-state PL Si-CDs composites was synthesized via one-step solvothermal treatment of citric acid, dicyandiamide and 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTES). Second, to obtain good compatibility, two types of silicone dendrimers (denoted as D1 and D2) were designed and synthesized via transesterification and hydrosilylation reaction method as waveguide materials. The structure difference between D1 and D2 is the content of phenyl and methoxy. Remarkably, high loading of Si-CDs (up to 35 wt%) in silicone dendrimers could be realized owing to their good compatibility and abundant nano cavities. Furthermore, single-layered LSCs could be prepared easily by direct casting the above mixtures and the followed room temperature curing on glass substrates due to their low viscosity. More importantly, the external optical efficiency (η opt) of 7.58%, 5.38% had been achieved by D1 and D2 based LSCs (25 × 25 × 1 mm), respectively. Different molecular structure of silicone dendrimers (D1 and D2) would realize controlled performances (e. g. optical and efficiency), posing great potential in various specific applications.

Materials: Phenyltrimethoxysilane was purchased from Dalian Yuanyong Organosilicon Plant and used after distillation. Vinyltrimethoxysilane and 1,1,3, 3-tetramethyldisiloxane were provided by Aladdin Chemistry Co., Ltd and used after distillation. Citric acid (CA, 99%), activated charcoal and 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTES, 98%) were purchased from Aladdin Chemistry Co., Ltd and used as received. Dicyandiamide (DCD, 98%) was obtained from Shanghai Aladdin Bio-Chem Technology Co., Ltd and used as received. Analytic reagents of tetrahydrofuran (THF), ethanol (EtOH), bromobenzene, anhydrous sodium sulfate, acetic acid and acetic anhydride were supplied by Sinopharm Group Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd and used as received. Magnesium (AR) was purchased from Sinopharm Group Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd and used after disposing. Trifluoromethanesulfonic acid (TfOH, 98%) was purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd and used as received. Platinum (0) 1,3-divinyl-1,1,3, 3-tetramethyldisiloxane (theoretical Pt content 20%) was purchased from Umicore Metal International Trade (Shanghai) Co., Ltd, and diluted with 1,3-divinyl-1,1,3, 3-tetramethyldisiloxane with a Pt content of 10000 ppm. Tyzor® 722 titanate chelate was obtained from Guangzhou Jianyi chemicals Imp & Exp Co., Ltd and used as received.

Synthesis of Si-CDs: CA (0.57 g), dicyandiamide (2.5 g) and APTES (3.47 mL) were dissolved in the mixture of 25 mL of H2O and 5 mL of EtOH and stirred at room temperature for 30 min. The obtained solution was then transferred into a 50 mL teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave, heated to 200℃, and maintained at this temperature for 12 h. After cooling to room temperature, precipitates were collected by centrifuging the above adduct and washed with H2O and EtOH for three times. Finally, the yellow-green emissive Si-CDs were obtained by the precipitates under vacuum at 60°C overnight. For a comparison, the CDs was prepared by the same process as Si-CDs without the addition of APTES. And the CDs powder was collected by rotary evaporator and dried in vacuum at 60°C overnight.

Synthesis of vinylphenyldimethoxysilane: 24 g of magnesium chips and 50 g of THF were added to a four necked round bottom flask equipped with a magnetic stirrer, a thermometer, a condenser and a dropping funnel. The mixture was stirred at 35℃, during which bromobenzene (3 g) was added into the flask to start-up the reaction gently, while the mixture of bromobenzene (147 g) and THF (65 g) were added dropwise in 2 h. Then the temperature of oil bath was raised to 70℃ and kept for 2 h. After reaction, the mixture was cooled to room temperature and the THF solution of phenyl magnesium bromide was obtained by suction filtration. The THF solution of phenyl magnesium bromide was then added to a four necked round bottom flask through a dropping funnel within 2 h, in which 250 g of vinyltrimethoxysilane was added previously. The mixture was refluxed and stirred at 60℃. Then the temperature of oil bath was raised to 75℃ and kept at this temperature for 1 h. After cooling to room temperature, the reaction mixture was treated by suction filtration and the residue was washed with 60 g of vinyltrimethoxysilane·THF and excess vinyltrimethoxysilane were removed by consecutive atmospheric distillation. Finally, the colorless liquid was collected at 60℃ (1 mmHg) by vacuum distillation and proved to be vinylphenyldimethoxysilane (102 g). IR (cm−1): Si-Ph 1429, 1594, Si-O-CH3 2837, 1189, 1084, Si-Vi 3071, 3053, 1404, 967, 549. 1H NMR (400 MHz, Chloroform-d) δ (ppm): 7.67 – 7.61 (7.64 m, 2H), 7.47 – 7.35 (7.40 m, 3H), 6.26 – 6.08 (6.18 m, 2H), 5.97 (dd, J = 19.7, 4.8 Hz, 1H), 3.59 (s, 6H).

Synthesis of phenyltris(dimethylsiloxy)silane: Phenyltrimethoxysilane (99 g), 1,1,3,3-tetramethyldisiloxane (120.6 g) and TfOH (0.48 g) were taken into a four necked round bottom flask equipped with a magnetic stirrer, a thermometer, a condenser and a dropping funnel, the mixture was stirred at 50℃. Acetic acid (90 g) was added through the dropping funnel in 20 min and stirred for another 15 min. Then acetic anhydride (75.74 g) was added dropwise in 20 min and stirred for another 50 min at 50℃. The reaction mixture was cooled to room temperature and transferred to a 500 mL separating funnel, washed with deionized water for 6–7 times until the separated lower water layer was neutral. The upper layer was then transferred to a beaker and dried with anhydrous sodium sulfate (10 g) overnight. After filtration, the filtrate was first atmospheric distilled to remove excess 1,1,3, 3-tetramethyldisiloxane and then vacuum distilled at 70℃ (1 mmHg) to collect the colorless liquid (134 g), which was proved to be phenyltris(dimethylsiloxy)silane. IR (cm−1): Si-H 2132, 899, Si-Ph 1430, 1594, Si-CH3 2961, 1254, Si-O-Si 1065. 1H NMR (400 MHz, Chloroform-d) δ (ppm): 7.68 – 7.51 (7.57 m, 2H), 7.47 – 7.29 (7.36 m, 3H), 4.78 (4.78, J = 2.7 Hz, 3H), 0.22 (d, J = 2.9 Hz, 18H). 29Si NMR (119 MHz, Chloroform-d) δ (ppm): −4.66 (s, 3Si), −75.55 (s, 1Si).

Synthesis of silicone dendrimers: In a four necked round bottom flask equipped with a magnetic stirrer, a thermometer, a condenser and a dropping funnel, vinyltrimethoxysilane (66.6 g) and platinum (0) 1,3-divinyl-1,1,3, 3-tetramethyldisiloxane (0.09 g) were added. The mixture was stirred at 55℃ under nitrogen. Phenyltris(dimethylsiloxy)silane (49.5 g) was then added through the dropping funnel in 60 min. After the reaction mixture was stirred at 75℃ and 90℃ each for 2 h, vacuum distillation was carried out under high vacuum (1 mm Hg) to ensure complete removal of excess starting materials. After cooled to room temperature, activated charcoal (2 g) was added into the flask and stirred for 2 h. Filtration was performed to collect the colorless liquid silicone dendrimer (D1, 112 g). Refractive index (25℃): 1.4545. IR (cm−1): Si-Ph 1430, 1594, Si-CH3 2944, 1252, Si-O-CH3 2839, 1189, 1088. 1H NMR (400 MHz, Chloroform-d) δ (ppm): 7.37 – 7.28 (m, 3H), 3.56 – 3.51 (m, 27H), 1.15 – 1.07 and 0.63 – 0.51 (m, 12H), 0.18 – 0.09 (m, 18H). 29Si NMR (119 MHz, Chloroform-d) δ (ppm): 9.39 (d, 3Si J = 36.0 Hz), −42.13(s 3Si), −76.10 – −80.33 (m 1Si).

Silicone dendrimer D2 (133 g) was obtained through similar strategy to D1 by replacing vinyltrimethoxysilane (66.6 g) with vinylphenyldimethoxysilane (87.3 g). Refractive index (25℃): 1.5085. IR (cm−1): Si-Ph 1429, 1592, Si-CH3 2955, 1255, Si-O-CH3 2835, 1189, 1084. 1H NMR (400 MHz, Chloroform-d) δ (ppm): 7.75 – 7.57 (m, 8H), 7.56 – 7.36 (m, 12H), 3.74 – 3.60 (m, 18H), 1.18, 0.92 – 0.78 and 0.75 – 0.45 (m, 12H), 0.24 – 0.04 (m, 18H). 29Si NMR (119 MHz, Chloroform-d) δ (ppm): 9.71 (d, 3Si J = 34.5 Hz), −16.42 (d, 3Si J = 76.2 Hz), −77.91 (d, 1Si J = 44.6 Hz).

Preparation of LSCs: Certain amount of Si-CDs and silicone dendrimer were first mixed in a 5 mL beaker and stirred for 30 min, second, a certain amount of catalyst (Tyzor® 722) was added into the above mixture and stirred for another 5 min. Then, 200 µL of mixture were taken by a pipette and dropped on the glass substrate (25 × 25 × 0.7 mm), 800 µL of mixture were dropped on the larger glass substrate (50 × 50 × 0.7 mm). The suspension was spread out uniformly on the glass substrate and a uniform film was formed by room temperature curing for 48 h. The detailed formulation was listed in Table S1.

Characterization: Fourier transformed infrared spectrometry (FTIR) was carried out on a Thermo Scientific NICOLET iS50 spectrometer using KBr pellets. 1H NMR spectra was recorded at room temperature by a Bruker Avance 400 MHz nuclear magnetic resonance spectrometer, 29Si NMR spectra was recorded at room temperature by a Bruker Avance 600 MHz nuclear magnetic resonance spectrometer. The morphology and size of Si-CDs were characterized by field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM, Zeiss Sigma 500, GRE) under an accelerating voltage of 10 kV. The size distributions of CDs in the Si-CDs were measured by using the high-resolution field-emission transmission electron microscope (HRTEM, FEI, Czech). The refractive index of D1, D2 and suspension of D2 and 5 wt% Si-CDs were measured by Abbe refractometer under sunlight at 25℃. Based on this, the refractive index of Si-CDs was obtained by calculation according to the previous work [40]. The fluorescence emission spectrum was measured by a fluorescence spectrophotometer (F-320, Tianjin Gangdong Sci&Tech Co., Ltd). The UV–vis absorption spectra of Si-CDs and LSCs were collected with an UV–vis absorption spectrometer (UV-3600 Plus, Shimadzu, Japan). The transmittance spectra of LSCs were collected with an UV–vis transmittance spectrometer (UV-3600 Plus, Shimadzu, Japan). The luminescence decay curves were obtained on a fluorescence spectrophotometer (PicoQuant Fluo Time 300). The PLQY of Si-CDs and LSCs were measured on fluorescence spectrophotometer (PicoQuant Fluo Time 300) with a calibrated integrating sphere. The Si-CDs powders were spread in the groove of the glass sheet, and compacted with another glass sheet to press flat, and then the two glass sheets were insert in sample pool for the PLQY and lifetime measurement. The current density–voltage (J-V) curves of the silicon solar cells were measured by a source meter (Keithley 2400, USA) under simulated AM 1.5 G (100 mW/cm2 irradiance) provided by a solar simulator (Newport Oriel Sol 3A Class AAA, 64023A), silicon solar cells (Voc : 0.48 V, Jsc : 41.28 mA/cm2, PCE:14%, EQE:16%) was purchased from Ningbo Angui Electronics Co., Ltd.

In this work, the LSC was prepared by first dispersing Si-CDs in the silicone dendrimers and further forming a polymer composite film on the glass substrate (Fig. 1 ). On one hand, the Si-CDs was designed via protecting CDs by silicone, aiming to conquer the aggregation-caused fluorescence quenching in the conventional CDs-based LSCs. On the other hand, silicone dendrimers were designed to match Si-CDs for realizing good compatibility since there is the same component, e. g., silicone, between both Si-CDs and silicone dendrimers. Based on this architecture, LSCs with high performances could be achieved since high loading of Si-CDs in silicone dendrimers could be easily realized. By contrast, the conventional CD-based LSCs could not obtain high concentration of CDs in LSC waveguide matrix, limiting the development of high performance LSCs.

As shown in Fig. 4 , several intermediates were synthesized to prepare silicone dendrimers (D1 and D2). Firstly, phenylsiltriol was synthesized by the transesterification reaction between methoxy groups of phenyltrimethoxysilane and acetic acid under of the catalysis TfOH. In this step, the byproduct was methyl acetate, and the reaction is exothermic. Subsequently, phenyltris(dimethylsiloxy)silane was successfully synthesized after the capping agent 1, 1, 3, 3-tetramethyldisiloxane was added into the mixture to react with phenylsiltriol. In order to increase yield of the reaction, excess 1, 1, 3, 3-tetramethyldisiloxane was added before the reaction was started. Meanwhile, acetic anhydride was adopted due to its capability of consuming the by-product water, which can facilitate polycondensation of phenylsiltriol and reduce the yield of phenyltris(dimethylsiloxy)silane [48]. FTIR spectra (Fig. S4, 2132 cm−1), together with 1H NMR (Fig. S5, δ: 4.78 ppm) and 29Si NMR (Fig. S6, δ: −4.66 ppm) all showed the typical Si-H of phenyltris(dimethylsiloxy)silane confirming that phenyltris(dimethylsiloxy)silane was obtained successfully. Vinylphenyldimethoxysilane was prepared by vinyltrimethoxysilane reaction with Grignard’s reagent phenylmagnesium bromide [49]. Finally, phenyltris(dimethylsiloxy)silane was reacted with vinyltrimethoxysilane and vinylphenyldimethoxysiliane respectively via hydrosilylation reaction under Pt catalysis without any solvent [50]. After purification by vacuum distillation (under 90°C/1mmHg) and activated charcoal adsorption of the catalyst, silicone dendrimers D1 and D2 were obtained [51]. As are shown in the spectra of FTIR (Fig. S7 and S8), 1H NMR (Fig. S9) and 29Si NMR (Fig. S10) of D1 and D2, there were no characteristic peaks of Si-H or Si-Vi. D1 and D2 were obtained successfully.

As shown in Fig. 5 , single-layered D1/D2 based LSCs were prepared by direct casting the suspension of Si-CDs and silicone dendrimers on glass substrate followed by curing at room temperature for 48 h. During the curing process, the films were formed by cross-linking reaction between methoxy groups of the silicone dendrimers under the catalyst and moisture in the air. Compared to conventional polymer matrix, such as PMMA or PDMS, the D1/D2 system showed more advantages, on one hand, the low viscosity could be obtained although a mass of Si-CDs were dispersed in the silicone dendrimers, closely relying on their molecular structures, such as the abundance of cavities in the silicone dendrimers. On the other hand, there is good compatibility between Si-CDs and silicone dendrimers based on their same component, e.g., silicone. The unique feature of the D1/D2 system would lead to facile and rapid film-forming method (e.g., simply direct casting method), easy large-scale production, and high loading of Si-CDs in D1/D2 based LSCs.

The D1 and D2 based LSCs under daylight and UV light are presented in Fig. 6 a and b, respectively. With the increase of Si-CDs concentrations, the D1 and D2 based LSCs show the gradual optical transformation from transparent to translucent. By comparison, D2 based LSCs have higher transparency, which had further been confirmed by the transmittance spectra in Fig. 7 a and b. Clearly, the transmittance of the D2 based LSCs with the Si-CDs concentration from 0 to 35 wt% are at 88%, 73%, 62%, 57%, 35%, 32%, 11% and 6% at 700 nm wavelength, while the D1 based LSCs showed similar transmittance below 20%.

The difference of transparency in D1 and D2 based LSCs is probably from the refractive index contrast between silicone dendrimers and Si-CDs. D1 and D2 have various refractive index owing to their different molecular structure. D2 has more phenyl groups than D1, leading to the higher refractive index of D2 (1.5085) than D1 (1.4545). D2 based LSCs are more transparent than D1 based LSCs because of the refractive index matching between D2 and Si-CDs since the refractive index of Si-CDs is 1.568 (25℃). Various transparency would lead to different performance of LSCs, thus realizing tunable performances of LSCs via simply controlling the structures of waveguide polymer matrix. In addition, the D1 and D2 based LSCs show similar trend on absorbance intensity (Fig. S11), the absorbance intensity increased gradually when the Si-CDs concentrations are changed from 0 to 35 wt%, D1 based LSCs show higher absorbance intensity than that of D2-based LSCs. More importantly, the D1 and D2 based LSCs show uniform and bright yellow-green emission under illumination of 365 nm of UV light, as shown in Fig. 6a and b. The uniform emission on the entire LSCs proves that good dispersion of Si-CDs in silicone dendrimers based on the compatibility between both. With the increase of Si-CDs content, brighter yellow-green emission is more obvious, which also could be proven in Fig. 7c and d. The D1 and D2 based LSCs show similar trend on PL intensity, the PL intensity increases gradually when the Si-CDs concentrations were changed from 0 to 35 wt%. At 35 wt% Si-CDs concentration, the maximum PL intensity of D1 and D2 based LSCs could be obtained. The PLQYs of D1 and D2 based LSCs with Si-CDs content at 5 wt%, 15 wt%, and 30 wt% are listed in Table S2, with the increase of Si-CDs content, the PLQYs of both the LSCs relatively decreased. However, the fluorescence lifetime of D1 and D2 based LSCs is basically stable at about 6 ns (Fig. S12 and Table S3). The results above demonstrate that the Si-CDs can conquer their aggregation-caused fluorescence quenching even at high loading content.

Fig. 8 a and b shows the typical J–V characteristic curves of the D1 and D2 based LSCs with different concentrations of Si-CDs from 0 to 35 wt%. The results indicate that all LSCs have the same trends: the photovoltages and the photocurrent density obviously increase with the increasing of Si-CDs concentrations and reach the maximum value at 35 wt% of Si-CDs content. In detail, the photovoltaic parameters of the D1 based LSCs are listed in Table S4, values of V oc, J sc, FF, η opt, and η could reach up to 0.48 V, 19.56 mA/cm2, 63.92%, 7.58% and 6.00% at Si-CDs content of 35 wt%, respectively (Calculation methods are listed in the Supporting Information and the photographs of the produced LSCs under AM 1.5 G radiation are shown in Fig. S13). Compared with the control LSCs at Si-CDs content of 0 wt% (1.33%), the external optical efficiency has been increased by 4.7 times. Similarly, D1 based LSCs with 5 wt%, 10 wt%, 15 wt%, 25 wt% and 30 wt% have the η opt of 2.67%, 3.99%, 5.07%, 5.47% and 6.71%, respectively (Fig. 8c and Table S4). Meanwhile, from the photovoltaic parameters of the D2 based LSCs in Table S5, values of V oc, J sc, FF, η opt, and η could reach up to 0.46 V, 13.88 mA/cm2, 60.41%, 5.38% and 3.86% at 35 wt% Si-CDs content, respectively. Compared with the control LSCs at Si-CDs content of 0 wt% (1.27%), the external optical efficiency has been increased by 3.2 times (Fig. 8d and Table S5). The η opt of 2.04%, 2.40%, 2.54%, 2.93% and 4.39% could be obtained in D2 based LSCs with 5 wt%, 10 wt%, 15 wt%, 25 wt% and 30 wt% had respectively (Fig. 8d and Table S5). Although small Stokes shift and scattering effect from Si-CDs would affect the efficiencies of LSCs, higher external optical efficiencies also had been achieved, probably resulting from the more absorption and enhanced PL emission with Si-CDs concentrations. Furthermore, the enhanced absorption and PL emissions with Si-CDs concentrations had been proved in Fig. 7 and Fig. S11. Although the Si-CDs form an aggregation in the LSCs system (the cross-sectional FESEM images of D1 and D2 based LSCs, Fig. S14), high concentration of Si-CDs still lead to high performance of LSCs, which rarely happened in conventional CDs based LSCs. Herein, solid-state PL Si-CDs play a key role, which greatly conquer the aggregation-caused fluorescence quenching of conventional CDs [27,52–53]. By comparison, D1 based LSCs show higher external optical efficiency than that of D2 based LSCs at all Si-CDs contents, probably resulting from more absorption of D1 system owing to the lack of transparency. When the LSCs samples were placed at room temperature for two months, there is slightly decline in the η opt of the D1 and D2 based LSCs (Fig. S15 and S16). The results showed that the D1 and D2 based LSCs have good stability because there still remain at least 83% of η opt. The η opt of the larger LSCs (50 × 50 × 1 mm) based on D1 and D2 at Si-CDs content of 35 wt% are 3.51% and 2.33% (Fig. S17). In general, the η opt of the LSCs based on silicone dendrimers and Si-CDs in this work are relatively high comparing to other similar works (Table S6). Although high Si-CDs concentration would lead to high performances of LSCs, the transparency of LSCs also would be affected. Thus, the specific applications at various LSCs conditions would be further explored for broadening the applications of LSCs.

In summary, novel single-layered LSCs are prepared based on solid-state PL Si-CDs as luminescent species and silicone dendrimers (D1 and D2) as matrix materials by simply casting technique. High loading of Si-CDs (up to 35 wt%) in D1 and D2 based LSCs could be obtained owing to the good compatibility between silicone dendrimers and Si-CDs, low viscosity, rich cavity. Furthermore, increased Si-CDs loading would lead to higher performance of LSCs, relying on the conquering aggregation-caused solid-state fluorescence quenching feature of Si-CDs. The maximum external optical efficiency of 7.58% for D1 based LSCs (25 × 25 × 1 mm) has been obtained, an increase by nearly 4.7 times could be achieved compared to the control LSC. Importantly, various molecular structure design of silicone dendrimers (D1 and D2) would lead to different performances of LSCs, e. g., transparency and η opt, potentially expanding the applications of LSCs.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the Ministry of Science and Technology of China (Grant No. 2016YFA0200200), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant Nos. 51673060, 11574075).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2021.130158.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
